title: Memory Supercycle — Most Severe DRAM Shortage in 15 Years
catalyst: Goldman Sachs raised its 2026 DRAM supply-demand gap forecast from 3.3% to 4.9% — the most severe shortage in 15 years. SK Hynix reported HBM, DRAM, and NAND capacity is 'essentially sold out' for 2026, Samsung and SK Hynix raised HBM3E prices ~20% for 2026, and DDR5 contract prices have surged 100%+ to $19.50 per unit. Micron Q2 FY2026 posted $23.86B revenue (+196% YoY) and guided Q3 to $33.5B with 81% gross margins — what would be the most profitable quarter in semiconductor history. Both Samsung and SK Hynix warn shortages extend through 2027 and beyond as hyperscaler customers pre-book multi-year supply.
thesis: Hyperscaler AI capex confirmed at ~$725B in Q1 2026 earnings is colliding with structural memory undersupply: HBM consumes 23% of DRAM wafers in 2026 (up from 19%) which starves conventional DRAM, and the bit-density gains that historically deflated memory prices have flattened just as Google TPU, Amazon Trainium, and NVIDIA Rubin ramps all demand HBM3E/HBM4. This is not the typical 18-month memory cycle — it is a multi-year capacity-constrained regime in which the marginal customer is paying anything to secure supply. US-listed primary memory producers are the cleanest beneficiaries since pricing flows directly to margin, and most still trade at single-digit-to-low-teens forward earnings reflecting an old commodity-cycle framework that no longer applies.

ticker_rationale.MU: Micron is the only US-listed HBM producer and the third leg of the HBM oligopoly with Samsung and SK Hynix — its HBM4 is ramping into NVIDIA Vera Rubin shipments through 2026 with HBM4e targeting 2027. At 7.9x forward P/E with 81% guided gross margins and $33.5B in next-quarter revenue, the stock is still being priced like a cyclical commodity name despite contracts pre-booked through 2026 and pricing power that more closely resembles a duopoly than a commodity [Micron Q2 FY2026 results, Goldman Sachs DRAM forecast].
ticker_rationale.SNDK: Sandisk is the purest Western-listed NAND pure-play after the WDC spinoff — Q2 FY2026 revenue grew 61% YoY with gross margins hitting 51% (a 20-point improvement from late-WDC years) and Q3 guidance calling for $4.4B+. As HBM consumes DRAM wafer capacity and AI workloads shift cold/warm data storage to high-capacity NAND, hyperscalers are signing long-term supply contracts extending through 2028 [Sandisk Q2 FY2026 results, Tom's Hardware on memory shortages].

risks: Memory is historically the most cyclical sector in semiconductors — even within a supercycle, inventory corrections of 30-50% drawdowns are common, and the stocks often roll over before the cycle officially ends

Samsung and SK Hynix are bringing additional HBM4 capacity online through 2027 — if hyperscaler order patterns even modestly normalize, the marginal supplier (Micron, with the smallest share) is hit first
AI training demand could shift toward more memory-efficient architectures (mixture-of-experts, sparse models) that reduce HBM intensity per unit of compute
Trade tensions or export controls on memory could disrupt hyperscaler procurement patterns and create inventory whipsaw effects
If a hyperscaler aggressively diversifies into custom HBM designs or in-house memory development (as Google has explored), it could compress pricing power for incumbent suppliers
sources: Goldman Sachs 2026 DRAM supply/demand forecast — 4.9% deficit, most severe in 15 years, 2026
